news_summary: Advancements in aviation technology are highlighted by innovative projects improving flight efficiency and integration, alongside a remarkable rediscovery of an ancient Greek manuscript enriching historical knowledge.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine de la technologie aéronautique sont mis en avant par des projets innovants visant à améliorer l'efficacité et l'intégration des vols, parallèlement à la redécouverte remarquable d'un manuscrit grec ancien qui enrichit les connaissances historiques.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la tecnología aeronáutica se ven reflejados en proyectos innovadores que mejoran la eficiencia y la integración de los vuelos, junto con un notable redescubrimiento de un antiguo manuscrito griego que enriquece el conocimiento histórico.

articles.title: About Low Boom Flight Demonstrator (LBFD) Project
articles.summary: The Low Boom Flight Demonstrator project (LBFD) is part of NASA’s effort to help enable new aircraft noise standards that are required to open the market to commercial supersonic flight over land. The federal government banned all civilian supersonic flights over land more than fifty years ago due to sonic boom noise. If new standards are established, the U.S. aviation […]
articles.content:
NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft cruises above Palmdale and Edwards, California, during its first flight, Tuesday, Oct. 28, 2025. The aircraft traveled to NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California. NASA/Lori Losey

The Low Boom Flight Demonstrator project (LBFD) is part of NASA’s effort to help enable new aircraft noise standards that are required to open the market to commercial supersonic flight over land.

The federal government banned all civilian supersonic flights over land more than fifty years ago due to sonic boom noise. If new standards are established, the U.S. aviation industry can position itself to lead the commercial supersonic market, and passengers will benefit from significantly shorter travel times.

Over the past decade, fundamental research and experimentation have demonstrated the possibility of supersonic flight with greatly reduced sonic boom noise – one of several key areas needed to transform commercial supersonic flight.

NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft sits on a ramp at Lockheed Martin Skunk Works in Palmdale, California, during sunset. The one-of-a-kind aircraft is powered by a General Electric F414 engine, a variant of the engines used on F/A-18 fighter jets. The engine is mounted above the fuselage to reduce the number of shockwaves that reach the ground. The X-59 is the centerpiece of NASA’s Quesst mission, which aims to demonstrate quiet supersonic flight and enable future commercial travel over land – faster than the speed of sound. Lockheed Martin Corporation/Garr

The LBFD project will demonstrate a reduced sonic boom by utilizing a purpose-built experimental aircraft designated the X-59.

The LBFD project supports a multi-phase effort aimed at demonstrating the X-59’s ability to fly supersonic without generating loud sonic booms. The LBFD project leads Phase 1 of the Quesst mission, involving the design, fabrication, ground tests, and checkout flights of the X-59.

After ensuring the aircraft is safe and performing as expected, the LBFD project will support the rest of the mission team during Phase 2 to prove the aircraft is producing a quiet sound to people on the ground and is safe for operations in the National Airspace System.

At the conclusion of Phase 2, the X-59 aircraft will transfer to the Integrated Aviation Systems Program’s Flight Demonstrations and Capabilities project.
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Low Boom Flight Demonstrator project, which aims to enable commercial supersonic flight over land by reducing sonic boom noise. This represents a significant technological breakthrough with broad potential benefits for the aviation industry and passengers, including shorter travel times and opening new markets. The article provides detailed context about the project phases, the X-59 aircraft, and its innovative design, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world impact and clear substance.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s X-59 aims to make quiet supersonic flights over land possible
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over 50 years, civilian airplanes have not been allowed to fly faster than sound over land in the U.S. because of the loud sonic booms they create. These booms are noise waves that can disturb people on the ground.

**What happened** – NASA is working on a project called the Low Boom Flight Demonstrator (LBFD) to change this. They built a special airplane called the X-59 that is designed to fly faster than sound but create a much quieter sonic boom. The X-59 had its first flight in October 2025 near California. This plane uses a unique engine placement and shape to reduce the noise that reaches the ground.

**Impact** – If the X-59 proves it can fly quietly, new rules could allow commercial supersonic flights over land. This would let airlines offer much faster trips, cutting travel times significantly. It would also help the U.S. lead in new aviation technology and open up new travel options for passengers.

**What's next step** – NASA will continue testing the X-59 to make sure it is safe and quiet. After that, the aircraft will be used to show regulators and the public that supersonic flights can be done without loud booms. If successful, this could lead to new commercial supersonic planes flying over land in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s X-59 project is testing quiet supersonic flight to help bring faster, low-noise air travel over land closer to reality.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le X-59 de la NASA vise à rendre possibles les vols supersoniques silencieux au-dessus des terres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 50 ans, les avions civils ne sont pas autorisés à voler plus vite que le son au-dessus des terres aux États-Unis à cause des forts bangs soniques qu’ils produisent. Ces bangs sont des ondes sonores qui dérangent les personnes au sol.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA travaille sur un projet appelé Low Boom Flight Demonstrator (LBFD) pour changer cela. Ils ont construit un avion spécial nommé X-59, conçu pour voler plus vite que le son tout en créant un bang sonique beaucoup plus silencieux. Le X-59 a effectué son premier vol en octobre 2025 près de la Californie. Cet avion utilise un moteur placé de façon unique et une forme spéciale pour réduire le bruit au sol.

**Impact** – Si le X-59 démontre qu’il peut voler silencieusement, de nouvelles règles pourraient permettre des vols commerciaux supersoniques au-dessus des terres. Cela permettrait aux compagnies aériennes de proposer des voyages beaucoup plus rapides, réduisant considérablement les temps de trajet. Cela aiderait aussi les États-Unis à être leaders dans cette nouvelle technologie aéronautique et offrirait plus d’options de voyage aux passagers.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA va continuer à tester le X-59 pour s’assurer qu’il est sûr et silencieux. Ensuite, l’avion sera utilisé pour montrer aux autorités et au public que les vols supersoniques peuvent se faire sans bangs bruyants. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait ouvrir la voie aux avions commerciaux supersoniques volant au-dessus des terres dans le futur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le projet X-59 de la NASA teste le vol supersonique silencieux pour rapprocher le voyage aérien rapide et peu bruyant au-dessus des terres de la réalité.
articles.personality_title_es: El X-59 de la NASA busca hacer posibles los vuelos supersónicos silenciosos sobre tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 50 años, los aviones civiles no han podido volar más rápido que el sonido sobre tierra en EE. UU. debido a los fuertes estampidos sónicos que generan. Estos son ondas de ruido que molestan a las personas en el suelo.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA está trabajando en un proyecto llamado Low Boom Flight Demonstrator (LBFD) para cambiar esto. Construyeron un avión especial llamado X-59 que está diseñado para volar más rápido que el sonido pero crear un estampido sónico mucho más silencioso. El X-59 realizó su primer vuelo en octubre de 2025 cerca de California. Este avión usa una ubicación especial del motor y un diseño único para reducir el ruido que llega al suelo.

**Impacto** – Si el X-59 demuestra que puede volar en silencio, podrían establecerse nuevas reglas que permitan vuelos supersónicos comerciales sobre tierra. Esto permitiría a las aerolíneas ofrecer viajes mucho más rápidos, reduciendo significativamente los tiempos de vuelo. También ayudaría a EE. UU. a liderar en tecnología aérea y abriría nuevas opciones de viaje para los pasajeros.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA continuará probando el X-59 para asegurarse de que es seguro y silencioso. Luego, el avión se usará para mostrar a los reguladores y al público que los vuelos supersónicos pueden hacerse sin estampidos fuertes. Si tiene éxito, esto podría llevar a que aviones supersónicos comerciales vuelen sobre tierra en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – El proyecto X-59 de la NASA prueba vuelos supersónicos silenciosos para acercar los viajes aéreos rápidos y de bajo ruido sobre tierra a la realidad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek, futuristic supersonic aircraft with a distinctive elongated nose and elevated engine flying gracefully above a softly glowing desert landscape representing Palmdale and Edwards, California at sunset, with gentle sound waves radiating subtly downward, symbolizing quiet supersonic flight and innovation in aviation technology.

articles.title: Lost page of ancient Greek text by Archimedes resurfaces in France
articles.summary: One of three missing pages from Archimedes' palimpsest, a 10th century manuscript containing several copies of the Greek scientist's ancient texts, has been found by a researcher in the archives of a museum in the French city of Blois.
articles.content:
The Archimedes Palimpsest sits under plexiglass at the Field Museum in Chicago on November 3, 1999.

It all started off as a joke, a French researcher told AFP.

But what the team found was a piece of history – a long-lost page from a legendary manuscript by ancient Greek mathematician Archimedes which had been languishing, forgotten, in the archives of a French museum.

Archimedes, considered one of history's greatest mathematicians and inventors, lived in the third century BC in the city of Syracuse.

Among his many discoveries was the principle of buoyancy, which he struck while stepping out of a bath – famously prompting him to shout "Eureka!".

This treatise and many others of his lasted down through the centuries on a manuscript called a palimpsest, which changed hands many times.

A palimpsest is a handwritten parchment that has had its original text scraped off before being written over, sometimes several times.

Victor Gysembergh, the researcher at France's CNRS research centre who found the missing page of Archimedes's palimpsest, told AFP it was a "treasure trove of lost texts from antiquity".

Read more'Choose CNRS': French research centre seeks to attract US-based scientists

As well as Archimedes's mathematical breakthroughs, the manuscript contains his "philosophical, literary and religious" writings, Gysembergh enthused.

The palimpsest itself was not written by Archimedes's hand but was instead copied during the 900s AD.

Around two centuries later, the text was erased and re-used as a Christian prayer book.

From Constantinople to Bezos?

That was just the beginning of the journey for this unique manuscript, whose fate followed the twists and turns of history.

By the 1800s, it was held by the Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem, including inside a library in Constantinople, modern-day Istanbul.

Read moreThe Bright Side: Researchers glimpse early signs of human technology with oldest wooden tools

Danish historian Johan Ludvig Heiberg found the palimpsest there – and took photographs of every page in 1906.

However, at some point during World War I, the document vanished without a trace.

It somehow wound up in the private collection of a French family, which eventually put it up for auction in 1998.

It was purchased by an anonymous Western businessman.

Insiders quoted by Germany's Der Spiegel newspaper claimed it was Amazon billionaire Jeff Bezos, although the true identity remains unknown.

But three of the palimpsest's 177 pages had disappeared.

This is where Gysembergh comes in.

"I am interested in palimpsests because they are a way to discover lost texts," said the lead author of a study in the German Journal of Papyrology and Epigraphy.

Sometimes Gysembergh searches for palimpsests in the libraries of different cities for fun.

The lost Archimedes's page was discovered "due in part to a joke", he said.

One day he was chatting with his office colleagues, when he mentioned that the old kings of France had kept part of their library in the central French city of Blois.

"Hey, let's see if there's a palimpsest in Blois," he told his colleagues.

'Very unexpected'

Gysembergh was astonished to find a hit on Arca – an online catalogue of digitised manuscripts – in the city's museum of fine art.

"It was very unexpected to stumble upon a Greek manuscript," Gysembergh said.

"And even more so to find a 10th-century scientific treatise!"

The researcher then compared the pages to the photos of Archimedes's palimpsest taken in 1906.

The handwriting, the geometric figures, even the errors all perfectly matched, he said.

One side of the page contains Archimedes's treatise "On the sphere and the cylinder", which was the first time the surface area of a sphere and its volume were described in such detail.

On the other side is a newer drawing, which is thought to have been added in the 1900s in an attempt to increase the document's value.

To decipher the text below the drawing, Gysembergh hopes to carry out high-tech analysis – such as multispectral imaging and X-ray fluorescence – in the coming year.

He also hopes that this breakthrough will help find the other two missing pages of the palimpsest.

"Until this discovery, we had no reason to hope we would ever find them," he said.

"Now, if institutions or private collectors have this kind of manuscript, they should think about whether it could be one of the other lost pages."

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a lost page from Archimedes' palimpsest represents a significant breakthrough in historical and scientific knowledge, offering broad cultural and educational benefits. The find has meaningful impact as it revives ancient mathematical and scientific texts that can inspire and inform modern research and understanding. The article provides detailed context about the manuscript's history, significance, and future research plans, fulfilling the criteria for substance and scope.
articles.personality_title: Lost page from Archimedes' ancient manuscript found in France
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Archimedes was a famous Greek mathematician and inventor who lived over 2,000 years ago. Some of his important writings were copied onto a special kind of ancient book called a palimpsest. This book was reused many times, and parts of it went missing over the centuries.

**What happened** – A French researcher named Victor Gysembergh discovered one of the three missing pages from Archimedes' palimpsest in a museum archive in Blois, France. He found it by chance while looking through an online catalogue and confirmed the page matched photos taken more than 100 years ago.

**Impact** – This page includes detailed explanations of Archimedes' work on spheres and cylinders, which was groundbreaking in ancient mathematics. Finding it helps us better understand his discoveries and the history of science. It also shows that lost ancient texts can still be found, even after many years.

**What's next step** – The researcher plans to use special imaging technologies to read the faded text more clearly. He also hopes this find will encourage others to look for the two other missing pages, possibly in private collections or other libraries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A lost page from Archimedes' ancient manuscript has been rediscovered, opening new opportunities to study his ideas and recover more lost history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une page perdue du manuscrit ancien d'Archimède retrouvée en France
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Archimède était un célèbre mathématicien et inventeur grec qui a vécu il y a plus de 2 000 ans. Certains de ses écrits importants ont été copiés dans un type ancien de livre appelé palimpseste. Ce livre a été réutilisé plusieurs fois, et des parties ont disparu au fil des siècles.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un chercheur français nommé Victor Gysembergh a découvert une des trois pages manquantes du palimpseste d'Archimède dans les archives d'un musée à Blois, en France. Il l'a trouvée par hasard en consultant un catalogue en ligne et a confirmé que la page correspondait aux photos prises il y a plus de 100 ans.

**Impact** – Cette page contient des explications détaillées sur le travail d'Archimède concernant les sphères et les cylindres, une avancée majeure en mathématiques anciennes. Sa découverte nous aide à mieux comprendre ses découvertes et l'histoire des sciences. Elle montre aussi que des textes anciens perdus peuvent encore être retrouvés.

**Prochaine étape** – Le chercheur prévoit d'utiliser des technologies d'imagerie spéciales pour mieux lire le texte effacé. Il espère aussi que cette découverte encouragera d'autres à chercher les deux autres pages manquantes, peut-être dans des collections privées ou d'autres bibliothèques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une page perdue du manuscrit ancien d'Archimède a été retrouvée, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour étudier ses idées et récupérer une partie de l'histoire perdue.
articles.personality_title_es: Página perdida del antiguo manuscrito de Arquímedes encontrada en Francia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Arquímedes fue un famoso matemático e inventor griego que vivió hace más de 2,000 años. Algunos de sus escritos importantes fueron copiados en un tipo especial de libro antiguo llamado palimpsesto. Este libro fue reutilizado muchas veces, y partes de él se perdieron a lo largo de los siglos.

**Qué pasó** – Un investigador francés llamado Victor Gysembergh descubrió una de las tres páginas perdidas del palimpsesto de Arquímedes en los archivos de un museo en Blois, Francia. La encontró por casualidad mientras buscaba en un catálogo en línea y confirmó que la página coincidía con fotos tomadas hace más de 100 años.

**Impacto** – Esta página incluye explicaciones detalladas sobre el trabajo de Arquímedes con esferas y cilindros, que fue un avance importante en las matemáticas antiguas. Encontrarla ayuda a entender mejor sus descubrimientos y la historia de la ciencia. También muestra que todavía se pueden encontrar textos antiguos perdidos.

**Próximo paso** – El investigador planea usar tecnologías especiales de imagen para leer el texto desvanecido con más claridad. También espera que este hallazgo anime a otros a buscar las dos páginas perdidas restantes, posiblemente en colecciones privadas o en otras bibliotecas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una página perdida del antiguo manuscrito de Arquímedes ha sido redescubierta, abriendo nuevas oportunidades para estudiar sus ideas y recuperar más historia perdida.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate ancient parchment illuminated under soft light, featuring faded Greek geometric diagrams and handwritten text partially erased and overwritten, resting atop a wooden table scattered with old scientific instruments and a glowing magnifying glass, evoking discovery and the unveiling of lost knowledge.

articles.title: About Integrated Aviation Systems Program (IASP)
articles.summary: The Integrated Aviation Systems Program (IASP) conducts research and integrated, systems-level demonstrations in a flight environment to prove, mature and transition them into future aircraft and systems. The program aims to determine feasibility and accelerate development of less mature technologies, and for more mature technologies, execute highly complex flight demonstrations to prove and accelerate technology […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Crossflow Attenuated Natural Laminar Flow (CATNLF) scale-model wing flies for the first time on a NASA F-15 research jet during a test flight from NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California. The 75-minute flight confirmed the aircraft could maneuver safely with the approximately 3-foot-tall test article mounted beneath it. NASA will continue flight tests to collect data that validates the CATNLF design and its potential to improve laminar flow, reducing drag and lowering fuel costs for future commercial aircraft.

NASA/Carla Thomas
articles.reasoning: The article describes a successful flight test of a new wing technology by NASA that could improve laminar flow, reduce drag, and lower fuel costs for future commercial aircraft, representing a meaningful technological advancement with broad environmental and economic benefits.
articles.personality_title: NASA tests new wing design to make airplanes more fuel-efficient
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on new technologies to make airplanes use less fuel and fly more smoothly. One way to do this is by improving how air flows over the wings, which can reduce drag and save energy.

**What happened** – NASA attached a small, 3-foot wing model called the Crossflow Attenuated Natural Laminar Flow (CATNLF) wing to an F-15 research jet. The jet flew for 75 minutes at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in California. During this flight, the plane safely handled the extra wing part, allowing NASA to gather important data.

**Impact** – This test is important because it shows the new wing design can work in real flying conditions. Better airflow over wings means planes could use less fuel, which saves money and helps the environment by reducing pollution. This technology could be used in future commercial airplanes.

**What's next step** – NASA will keep flying tests with the CATNLF wing to collect more information. This will help engineers understand how well the design works and how it can be improved before it is used in real airplanes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s successful flight test of a new wing design brings us closer to more fuel-efficient and environmentally friendly airplanes.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA teste une nouvelle aile pour rendre les avions plus économes en carburant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur de nouvelles technologies pour que les avions consomment moins de carburant et volent plus efficacement. Améliorer le flux d’air sur les ailes peut réduire la traînée et économiser de l’énergie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a fixé un petit modèle d’aile de 3 pieds appelé Crossflow Attenuated Natural Laminar Flow (CATNLF) sous un avion de recherche F-15. L’avion a volé pendant 75 minutes au centre de recherche Armstrong en Californie. Pendant ce vol, l’avion a bien supporté cette aile supplémentaire, ce qui a permis de collecter des données importantes.

**Impact** – Ce test est important car il montre que la nouvelle aile peut fonctionner en conditions réelles de vol. Un meilleur écoulement de l’air sur les ailes signifie que les avions pourraient consommer moins de carburant, ce qui fait économiser de l’argent et réduit la pollution. Cette technologie pourrait être utilisée dans les avions commerciaux du futur.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA continuera les tests en vol avec l’aile CATNLF pour recueillir plus d’informations. Cela aidera les ingénieurs à mieux comprendre et améliorer le design avant son utilisation sur des avions réels.

**En une phrase** – Le test réussi d’une nouvelle aile par la NASA rapproche les avions plus économes en carburant et plus respectueux de l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA prueba un nuevo diseño de ala para hacer aviones más eficientes en combustible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está desarrollando nuevas tecnologías para que los aviones usen menos combustible y vuelen de manera más eficiente. Mejorar el flujo de aire sobre las alas puede reducir la resistencia y ahorrar energía.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA colocó un modelo pequeño de ala de 3 pies llamado Crossflow Attenuated Natural Laminar Flow (CATNLF) debajo de un avión de investigación F-15. El avión voló durante 75 minutos en el Centro de Investigación Armstrong en California. Durante el vuelo, el avión maniobró con seguridad con el ala extra, permitiendo recoger datos importantes.

**Impacto** – Esta prueba es importante porque muestra que el nuevo diseño de ala puede funcionar en condiciones reales de vuelo. Mejorar el flujo de aire sobre las alas significa que los aviones podrían usar menos combustible, lo que ahorra dinero y reduce la contaminación. Esta tecnología podría usarse en aviones comerciales futuros.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA continuará con más pruebas de vuelo con el ala CATNLF para recopilar más información. Esto ayudará a los ingenieros a entender mejor el diseño y mejorarlo antes de usarlo en aviones reales.

**Una frase clave** – La exitosa prueba de vuelo de la NASA acerca aviones más eficientes en combustible y amigables con el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek, stylized F-15 jet soaring gracefully above a softly colored desert landscape, with a prominent, gently glowing scale-model wing attached beneath it, symbolizing advanced aerodynamic innovation and smooth airflow through flowing, curved lines trailing behind the wing, rendered in natural earth tones and soft blues.
